Libertmes  had  not  yet  been  able  to  overturn. 

They  did  not,  however,  accept  of  their  defeat  and 
desist  from  the  war.  Baffled  in  this  front  attack, 
they  next  assailed  the  Reformer  on  the  flank.  "We 
have  too  many  ministers,"  said  they,  raismg  their 
voices  to  a  loud  pitch.  "  We  have  too  many 
ministers  and  too  many  sermons."  There  were 
then  only  four  pastors  in  Geneva ;  but  the  Liber- 
tines thought  that  they  were  four  too  many,  and 
although  they  cUd  not  demand  theii'  entire  sup- 
pression as  yet,  they  modestly  pi-oposed  that  they 
should  be  reduced  to  two.  As  regarded  the 
Churches,  they  would  not  lock  theii-  doors  out- 
right, but  they  would  at  once  abolish  the  sermon, 
in  which  their  vices  were  branded  with  a  pointed- 
ness  and  lashed  with  a.  severity  since  transferred 
from  the  pulpit  to  the  press  and  the  platform. 
They  were  willing  that  a  harmless  kind  of  wor- 
ship should  go  on.  They  would  permit  the  people 
to  be  taught  the  "  Creed,"  the  "  Lord's  Prayer," 
and  the  "Ten  Commandments."  This  amomit  of 
instruction,  they  thought,  might  be  safely  tolerated. 
As  to  those  floods  of  exposition  poured  forth  upon 
them  week-day  and  Sunday,  they  saw  no  need  for 
such :  it  was  dangerous ;  and  the  Council  ought 
to  raise  legal  dykes  within  which  to  confine  this 
tori-ent  of  pious  eloquence.^ 

The  Libertines  next  turned  theii-  attention  to  the 
correction  of  another  great  abuse,  as  they  deemed 
it.  The  liberty  of  the  press  foimd  no  favour  in 
the  eyes  of  these  champions  of  freedom.  What 
is  the  use,  they  asked,  of  so  many  Commentaries 
and  printed  books]  We  must  fetter  the  pen  of 
this  Calvin,  for  the  State  of  Geneva  is  not  able  to 
bear  the  many  books  he  is  sending  forth.  We 
must  stop  this  plenitude  of  writing  and  publishing.  =" 


Christ  estoit  servitude  miserable."  (Eoset,  torn,  iii.,  livr.

Such  was  theii-  estimate  of  that  mighty  genius,

triumphed  in  tliis  controversy. 

There  arose  yet  another  cause  of  complaint  and 
quarrel.  The  refugees  who  sought  asylum  in  Geneva 
were  at  this  time  increasing  from  week  to  week. 
Weeded  out  by  the  hand  of  persecution,  they 
were  the  men  of  tlie  purest  morals,  of  the  richest 
culture,  and  the  noblest  souls  which  the  surroiuid- 
ing  coimtries  could  boast.  Not  a  few  were  men 
of  the  highest  rank,  and  of  very  large  possessions, 
although  in  almost  every  case  they  arrived  pen- 
niless. *  The  little  State  began  to  inscribe  their 
names  on  the  registei-s  of  its  citizens.  The  proudest 
kingdom  would  have  done  itself  an  honour  by 
enrolling  such  men  among  its  subjects.  Not  so 
did  Perrin  and  his  faction  account  it.  "  They  are 
beggars  who  have  come  here  to  eat  the  bread  of 
the  Genevese " â€” so  did  they  speak  of  those  who 
had  foi-saken  all  for  the  Gospel â€” "they  are  Cal- 
\an's  allies,  who  flock  hither  to  support  him  in 
tyi-amiising  over  the  children  of  the  soil ;  they

and  destroying  the  liberties  of  the  townj  they 
are  the  enemies  of  the  republic,  aiid  what  so  likely 
as  that  they  will  purchase  their  way  back  into 
their  own  country  by  betraying  Geneva  to  the 
King  of  France?"  These  and  similar  accusations 
â€” the  ready  inventions  of  coarse  and  malignant 
natures â€” were  secretly  whispered  among  the  popu- 
lace, and  at  last  openly  preferred  before  the  Council, 
against  the  distinguished  men  of  almost  every 
nationality  now  assembled  in  Geneva. 

Early  in  the  year  1.555  the  matter  came  to  a 
head,  and  we  note  it  more  particularly  because 
it  brought  on  the  final  struggle  which  overtiu-ned 
the  faction  of  the  Libertines,  and  left  the  victory 
wholly  with  Calvin.  At  one  sitting  the  Council 
admitted  as  many  as  fifty  foreigners,  all  men  of 
kno^s^l  worth,  to  the  rights  of  citizenship.  Perrin 
and  his  followei-s  raised  a  louder  cry  than  ever. 
"  The  scum  of  Europe,"  "  the  supporters  of  Calvin's 
despotism,"  are  possessing  themselves  of  our  heri- 
tage.    These  were  the  epithets  by  which  they  chose

On  the  16th  May,  Perrin  returned  to  the  Council 
with  a  larger  number  of  followei-s,  chiefly  fishermen 
and  boatmen,  armed  with  huge  double-handled 
swoi'ds.-  This  motley  host  was  dismissed  wth  the 
same  answer  as  before.  The  malcontents  paraded 
the  streets  all  day,  calling  on  the  citizens  to  bestir 
themselves,  and  save  the  town,  which  was  on  the 
eve  of  being  sacked  by  the  foreigners.  The  better 
class  of  citizens  paid  no  attention  to  this  cry  of 
"The  wolf!"  and  remained  quiet  in  their  homes; 
but  the  ranks  of  the  rioters  were  swelled  by  num- 
bers of  the  lower  orders,  whose  patriotism  had 
been  stimulated  by  the  free  rations  of  wine  and 
food  which  were  served  out  to  them.' 

On  Friday,  the  18th  May,  the  heiids  of  the  party 
met  in  a  tavern  with  a  certain  number,  says  Boni- 
vard,  of  "  brawling  companions."  The  more 
moderate,  who  may  be  presumed  to  have  been  also 
the  more  sober,  wei'e  for  convoking  the  Council- 
General  ;  but  the  more  violent*  would  hear  of 
nothing  but  the  massacre  of  all  the  refugees  of 
religion,  and  their  supportei's.  The  Sunday  follow- 
ing, when  the  citizens  woidd  be  all  at  church,^  was 
fixed  on  for  the  execution  of  this  horrible  plot. 

The  eagerness  of  the  Libertines  to  consummate 
then-  crime  caused  the  plot  to  miscarry.  The  very 
next  night  after  their  meeting,  the  fumes  of  the 
wine,  we  may  charitably  believe,  not  having  as  yet 
exhaled,  the  mob-patriots  rushed  into  the  street 
with  arms  in  theii-  hands  to  begin  theii-  dreadful 
work.  "The  French,  the  French,"  they  shouted, 
"  are  taking  the  town  !  Slay  all,  slay  all ! "  But 
not  one  of  the  refugees  was  to  be  seen.  "  The 
Lord,"  says  Calvin,  "  had  poured  a  deep  sleep  upon

The  Council  assembled  in  a  few  days,  and  then 
measm-es  were  taken  to  bring  the  seditious  to  piuiLsh- 
ment,  and  prevent  the  peace  of  the  city  being 
broken  by  similar  outrages  in  time  to  come.  Four 
heads  fell  beneath  the  axe.  Periin's  also  would 
have  fallen,  had  he  not  timeously  care<l  for  its  safety 
by  flight.  With  him  fled  all  those  who  felt  that 
they  were  too  deeply  comjiromised  to  presume  on 
pardon.  The  rest  were  banished,  and  found  refuge 
on  the  tenitory  of  Bern.  The  issue  of  this  afiliis- 
determined  the  future  fortimes  of  Geneva. 

From  being  a  nest  of  Libertines,  who  woiild  have 
speetlily  wastt^d  their  own  and  their  city's  strength 
by  their  immoral  principles  and  theii-  disorderly 
lives,  and  who  woidd  have  plimge<.l  Geneva  into 
its  former  vassalage,  riveting  more  hopelessly  than 
ever  its  old  yoke  upon  its  neck,  this  small  but 
ancient  towir  was,  by  this  turn  of  afiliu-s,  rescued 
to  become  the  capital  of  Protestantism â€” the  metro- 
polis of  a  moral  em])ire. 

Here,  not  in  state,  like  a  Roman  cardinal,  but  in 
the  lowliness  of  a  simple  pastor,  dwelt,  not  the 
monarch  of  that  empu-e â€” for  monarch  it  has  not  on 
earth â€” but  the  jjresiding  mind,  the  dii'ecting  genius 
of  Protestantism.  From  this  centre  were  projja- 
gated  those  energies  and  influences  which,  mightier 
than  armies,  were  rending  the  shackles  from  the 
human  soiil,  and  calling  nations  from  theii"  tomb. 
Within  its  walls  the  elite  of  Europe  was  assem- 
bling ;  and  as  another  and  yet  another  illustrious 
stranger  presented  himself  at  its  gates,  and  crossed 
its  threshold,  the  brilliant  intellectual  glory  of 
Geneva  gathered  an  additional  brightness,  and 
its  moral  potency  waxed  stronger  day  by  day. 
To  it  all  eyes  were  turned,  some  in  admiration  and 
love,  others  in  hatred  and  fear.  Within  it  were 
born  those  great  thoughts  which,  sent  forth  in  let- 
ters, in  pamphlets,  in  gi-eat  tomes,  were  as  light 
to  roll  back  the  darkness â€” bolts  to  tliscomfit  the 
enemy,  and  pour  confusion  \ipon  the  champions 
of  error.  Protestant  troops  are  continually  pass- 
ing -out  at  its  gates,  girded  only  with  the  sword 
of  the  Spiiit,  to  assail  the  strongholds  of  dark- 
ness, and  add  new  provinces  to  the  kingdom  of 
the  Go.spel.  As  realm  after  realm  is  won,  there 
goes  forth  from    this  same  city  a  rescript  for  its

organisation  and  government ;  and  that  rescript 
meets  an  obedience  more  prompt  and  hearty  than

the  proud  mistress  of  the  ancient  world  for  the

subjugated. 

What  an  astouishing  phenomenon  must  the 
sudden  rise  of  tliLs  little  town  have  aj)peared  to 
the  men  of  those  times!  How  portentous  to  the 
friends  of  the  Old  religion !  It  had  not  been  built 
up  by  human  hands;  it  was  not  defended  by 
human  weapons  ;  yet  here  it  stood,  a  gi'eat  light- 
house in  the  centre  of  Christendom,  a  mother  of 
Churches,  a  nurse  of  martyrs,  a  school  of  evangelists, 
an  impregnable  asyliun  of  the  persecuted,  a  font  of 


ci\Tlisation,  an  abode  of  letters  and  arts,  a  rq^eat 
moral  tribunal,  where  the  actions  of  all  men  v.-ere 
weighed,  and  in  whose  inexorably  just  and  rigliteous 
awards  men  heard  the  voice  of  a  higher  tribunal, 
and  were  enabled  to  read  by  anticipation  the  final 
judgment  of  posterity,  and  even  that  of  the 
great  Supreme. 

This  was  what  Calvin's  -victory  had  brought  hiiii. 
He  might  well  deem  that  it  had  not  been  too 
dearly  bought.  Ti-uly  it  wa.s  worth  all  the 
anxieties  and  insults  he  had  borne,  all  the  toil 
and  agony  he  hatl  endiu'ed,  all  the  supplications 
and  tears  he  had  poured  out  to  achieve  it.  Nine 
years  had  he  been  in  gaining  it,  nme  years  were 
to  be  given  liim  to  turn  it  to  account.

Calvin  had  made  good  his  foothold  at  last.  He 
had  fought  for  this  little  town  as  conqueror  never 
fought  for  mightiest  empii-e,  and  now  it  was  his 
own.  Geneva  had  been  rescued  from  the  base 
uses  to  which  the  Libertines  had  destined  it,  and 
was  now  consecrated  to  the  noblest  of  all  ends. 
It  was  to  be,  not  the  head-quarters  of  a  philo- 
sophy that  would  have  demoralised  Christendom, 
but  the  temple  of  a  faith  that  was  to  regenerate 
and  exalt  it.  It  was  to  be,  not  the  beacon  to  lure 
to  the  whirlpool  of  revolution,  but  the  light  that 
would  guide  the  nations  to  the  haven  of  stability 
and  glory. 

The  Reformer  had  now  ix;ace.  But  liis  condi- 
tion can  be  justly  styled  peace  only  when  compared 
vnth  the  tempests  of  the  nine  previous  years.  Of 
these  he  had  feelingly  and  compendiouslv  said, 
"  that  while  everywhere  the  Church  w.as  agitatetl, 
at  Geneva  it  was  tos.sed  as  was  the  Ark  on  the 
billows."  It  was  a  true  description ;  but  the  calm 
had  come  at  last.  The  Ark  had  foimd  its  Ai-arat, 
and  now  v.ithin  that  city,  for  the  possession  of 
which  two  interests  had  so  stoutly  contended,  the 
fierce  mnds  had  gone  down,  and  the  waves  had 
subsided  into  rest. 

Calvin  now  proceeded   to  make  Geneva  fit  for 


the  grand  puiiioses  for  which  he  had  destined  her. 
And  Geneva  willingly  surrendered  herself  to  be 
fashioned  as  the  Reformer  wished;  her  life  she 
permitted  to  be  absorbed  in  his  life,  feeling  that 
with  him  was  inseparably  boimd  up  her  order, 
her  gi-andeur,  nay,  her  veiy  existence,  so  far  as 
concerned  every  good  and  useful  object.  Her 
law,  her  Council,  her  citizens,  all  tacitly  con- 
sented to  be  i>arts  of  the  gi-eat  Reformer â€” the 
ministries  through  wliich  he  operated  on  Christen- 
dom. "We  have  the  testimony  of  a  noble  eye- 
witness to  the  state  of  Geneva  at  this  period.  "  In 
my  heai-t,"  says  Knox,  in  a  letter  to  his  friend 
Mr.  Locke,  "  I  could  have  wished,  j'ea,  and 
cannot  cease  to  wish,  that  it  might  please  God 
to  guide  and  conduct  you  to  this  place,  where  I 
neither  fear  nor  eshame  to  say  is  the  most  jierfect 
school  of  Christ  that  ever  was  in  the  earth  since 
the  days  of  the  apostles.  In  other  places  I  confess 
Christ  to  be  truly  preached;  but  manners  and 
religion  to  be  so  sincerely  refoi-med,  I  have  not 
yet  seen  in  any  other  place  beside."'  Farel  bore 
similar  testimony  to   the    floiu-ishing  condition  of

HISTORY   OF   PROTESTANTISM. 


Geneva  after  its  many  perils.  "  I  was  lately  at 
Geneva,"  he  says,  "  and  so  deliglited  was  I  that  I 
could  scarce  tear  myself  away.  I  would  rather  be 
last  in  Geneva  than  first  in  any  other  place.  Were 
I  not  prevented  by  the  Lord,  and  by  my  love  for 
my  congregation,  nothing  would  hinder  me  from 
ending  my  days  there."  Dreliucourt  expressed  the 
same  admiration  a  hundred  years  after.' 

If  there  was  peace  in  the  days  of  Calvin  within 
Geneva,  there  were  ambushes  all  aromid.  The  fii'st 
trouble  was  created  by  the  banished  Libertines. 
Bern  took  the  part  of  these  exiles  in  the  quai-rel, 
declaring  that  they  had  been  guilty  of  no  crime, 
and  demanding  of  the  Council  and  citizens  of 
Geneva  that  they  should  give  satisfaction  to  those 
they  had  expelled,  and  receive  them  back.  It  may 
be  conjectured  that  there  was  in  all  this  a  little 
jealousy  on  the  part  of  the  jiowerful  Bern  of  the 
rising  glory  of  Geneva,  The  little  republic  replied 
to  this  haughty  demand  by  expelling  the  families 
of  the  Libertines,  and  forbiddmg  the  return  of  the 
banished  under  pam  of  death.  It  was  now  feared 
that  the  Libertines,  .supported  by  Bern,  meditated 
re-entering  Geneva  by  force  of  arms.  The  tenitory 
of  Bern  bordered  with  that  of  Geneva,  and  the 
Libertines  stationed  themselves  on  that  j^art  of  it 
which  lay  nearest  the  city,  and  offered  daUy 
menaces  and  petty  annoyances.  They  resorted  to 
the  bridge  of  the  Arve,  and  mocked  and  jeered  at 
the  Genevese  who  had  occasion  to  pass  that  way.' 
The  citizens,  in-itated  beyond  measure,  were  often 
on  the  point  of  rushing  out  and  pimishing  these 
insolences,  but  the  Council  restrained  them.'  The 
matter  continued  in  an  uneasy  and  dangerous  con- 
dition for  some  time,  but  a  sudden  turn  in  the 
politics  of  Europe,  which  menaced  both  cities  with 
a  common  danger,  brought  in  the  issue  deliverance 
to  Geneva. 

The  battle  of  St.  Quentin,  in  Normandy,  was 
fought  about  this  time.  In  this  fight  the  arms 
of  Charles  of  Spain  were  victorious  over  those  of 
Henry  II.  of  France.  Philibert  Emmanuel,  Prince 
of  Piedmont,  who  commanded  the  Spanish  army, 
was  the  heir  of  the  titles  and  rights  of  his  father 
Charles,  Duke  of  Savoy  ;  but  he  inherited  the  titles 
only ;  the  estates  had  gone  from  his  house,  and  were 
now  partly  in  the  hands  of  the  King  of  France, 
and  partly  in  possession  of  Bern,  and  other  Swiss 
cantons.  The  French  king  being  now  himibled, 
the  Prince  of  Piedmont  deemed  this  a  favourable 
moment  for  reclaiming   his   hereditary  dominions. 


He  issued  an  edict  to  that  effect,  and  immediately 
thereafter  dispatched  a  body  of  eight  thousand 
lanzknechts,  or  lancers,  to  establish  his  authority 
over  his  former  subjects.  The  alarm  was  gi-eat 
throughout  Switzerland,  and  more  especially  in 
Geneva  and  Bern.  The  Bernese  had  now  other 
things  to  think  of  than  the  quarrel  into  which  the 
banishetl  Liteitines  had  led  them.  Tliis  last 
matter  gradiiaUy  went  to  sleep;  and  thus  Geneva, 
by  this  shifting  in  the  great  European  â– ndnds,  was 
delivered  without  the  necessity  of  striking  a  single 
blow.' 

The  affiiii-s  of  Bolsec  and  Castalio  belong  to  bio- 
graphy rather  than  to  histoiy.  Both  of  these  men 
opposed  Calvin  on  the  doctrine  of  pi-edestmation. 
Both  of  them  inteiiiipted  him  publicl}'  when 
preaching  in  St.  Peter's.  The  Council  had  them 
seized,  on  the  ground  of  the  maintenance  of  the 
public  peace,  rather  than  on  the  ground  of  dif- 
ference of  doctrine.  The  residt  was  that  both 
were  bani.shed  from  Geneva,  never  to  return. 
This  pimishment,  which  has  been  laid  at  the 
door  of  the  Reformer,  has  been  denounced 
as  harsh.  But  we  ought  to  keep  in  mmd 
that  Bolsec  and  Castalio  were  not  Genevese, 
banished  from  then-  native  land ;  they  were 
foreigners  who  had  resided  in  Geneva,  the  one  a 
few  years,  the  other  only  a  few  months.'^  "As  to 
those  who  are  indignant  that  Bolsec  should  even 
have  been  banished,"  says  Bungener,  "  we  know 
not  what  to  riay  to  them,  unless  that  they  are  com- 
pletely ignorant  how  the  question  stood  in  regard 
to  the  Reformation  and  to  Geneva â€” especially  to 
Geneva.  To  wish  that  she  had  opened  her  gates 
to  all  the  vai-iations  and  daring  flights  of  reli- 
gious thought,  is  to  wish  that  that  gieat  lever,  the 
Refoi-mation,  had  without  a  fulcrum  lifted  the 
world." '' 

Stationed  just  outside  the  French  territory,  the 
Reformer  was  able,  from  this  citadel  in  which  God 
had  placed  him,  to  keep  constant  watch  over  the 
Protestant  Clnu-ch  of  France.  During  the  nine 
years  he  had  yet  to  live,  that  Church  was  the  object 
of  his  daily  cai-e.  He  had  found  her  in  her  cradle, 
and  he  nursed  her  into  strength.  It  was  for  his 
counsel  she  waited  when  any  emergency  arose,  and 
it  was  to  his  voice  and  pen  that  she  looked  for 
defence  when  danger  threatened.  She  revered  him 
as  her  father.

HISTORY   OF   PROTESTANTISM. 


The  first  necessity  of  Christendom,  in  the  opinion 
of  C'alvLu,  was  the  Gospel.  Accordingly,  it  â– vvas 
one  of  his  chief  labours  to  prepare,  in  the  school  of 
Gieneva,  c^ualified  preachers  who  would  go  forth, 
and  sow  everywhere  the  seed  of  the  kingdom. 
Many  of  these  missionaries  selected  France  as  their 
field  of  labour.  Thither  were  they  followed  by  the 
instructions  and  prayers  of  the  great  chief  from 
whose  feet  they  had  gone  forthj  and  the  conscious- 
ness that  his  eye  was  upon  them,  helped  to  make 
them  zealous  in  labour  and  courageous  in  death, 
which  so  many  of  them  were  called  to  endure  in 
the  discharge  of  their  ministry.  We  have  two 
proofs  that  gi-eat  numbers  offered  themselves  to 
this  most  inviting  but  very  hazardous  field.  Tlie 
first  is  the  letter  which  the  King  of  France,  Charles 
IX.,  in  January,  1.561,  sent  to  Geneva,  complain- 
ing of  the  preachers  who  had  come  from  thence, 
and  calling  upon  the  Council  to  recall  them.  The 
second  is  the  letter  of  Calvin  to  Bullinger,  in  the 
May  following,  which  reveals  incidentally  what  a 
powerful  projjaganda  Grcneva  had  become,  and 
shows  us  the  soldiers  of  the  Cross  daily  setting  out 
from  her  gates  to  spread  the  triumphs  of  the 
Gos^iel.  "  It  is  incredible,"  writes  Calvin,  "  with 
what  ardour  our  friends  devote  themselves  to  the 
spread  of  the  Gospel.  As  greedily  as  men  l^efore 
the  Pope  solicit  him  for  benefices,  do  they  ask  for 
employment  in  the  Churches  beneath  the  Cross. 
They  besiege  my  door  to  obtain  a  portion  of  the 
field  to  cultivate.  Never  had  monarch  courtiers 
more  eager  than  mine.  They  dispute  about  the 
stations  as  if  the  kingdom  of  Jesus  Christ  was 
peaceably  established  in  France.  Sometimes  I  seek 
to  restrain  them.  I  show  to  them  tlie  atrocious 
edict  which  orders  the  destruction  of  every  house 
in  which  Divine  .service  shall  have  been  celebrated. 
I  remind  them  that  in  more  than  twenty  towns  the 
faithful  have  been  massacred  by  the  populace."' 
In  those  happy  days â€” hajipy  although  stakes  were 
blaziaig â€” it  seemed  as  if  the  ancient  saying  was 
reversed,  and  that  no  longer  were  the  labourers  feu: 
No  wonder  that  Calvin  for  once  breaks  into  en- 
thusiasm, and  gives  vent  to  his  joy.  But  we  do 
the  Reformer  only  justice  when  we  say  that  he 
rejoiced  not  because  he  was  leader,  but  because  his 
soldiers  were  devoted.  They  were  men  worthy  of 
their  cajitain. 

The  success  of  these  Evangelists  entailed  new 
laboui-s  and  responsibilities  on  the  Reformer.  The 
Churches  which  they  planted  had  to  be  organised. 
These  new  communities  came  to  Geneva  for  the 
principles    of  their    constitution,  and    the   model 


of  then-  government.  If  Geneva  bore  the  like- 
ness of  Calvin,  France  now  began  to  bear  the 
likeness  of  Geneva.  Thus  the  cares  of  the  Re- 
fonner  were  multii)lied  and  his  labours  increased 
a.s  he  gi-ew  older.  He  lived  two  lives  in  one. 
The  Ufe  pa.ssed  in  commumon  with  God,  and 
in  the  study  of  His  Word,  in  his  closet,  fed  and 
sustained  that  other  life  of  intense  and  practical 
activity  which  he  led  before  the  world.  From 
the  contemplation  of  the  laws  of  the  kingdom 
of  Christ  as  laid  down  in  the  Bible,  he  rose  uj)  to 
apply  these,  as  he  believed,  in  the  arrangement  of 
li'\'ing  Churches,  and  in  the  scheme  of  jiolicy  which 
he  enjoined  on  the  now  powerful  Protestant  body 
of  France. 

His  counsels  on  this  head  expressed  a  lofty 
wisdom,  which  was  not  appreciated  at  the  time,  but 
the  three  centuries  that  have  since  elapsed  have  set 
their  seal  upon  it.  All  his  authority  and  eloquence 
were  put  forth  to  make  the  Protestants  eschew 
politics,  shun  the  battle-field,  and  continue  to  fight 
their  great  war  â– Hith  spiritual  weapons  only.  The 
Reformer  foresaw  for  the  ChiU'ch  of  France  a  glorious 
future,  if  only  she  shoidd  iiei-severe  in  this  path. 
He  had  no  faith  in  blood  shed  in  battle  :  no,  not 
in  A-ictorious  battle  ;  but  he  had  unbounded  faith 
in  blood  shed  at  the  stake  of  martyrdom.  Give  him 
martyrs â€” not  men  in  arms â€” and  Fi'ance  was  won. 
Not  one  letter  of  Calvin  is  extant  in  which  he 
recommends  a  contrary  course.  His  ad^dce  to  the 
Protestants  of  France  was  to  wait,  to  have  patience, 
to  submit  to  â– wrong,  to  abstain  from  revenging 
themselves,  and  not  to  be  sparing  of  then-  blood, 
for  every  droji  spilt  would,  he  assured  them,  bring 
them  nearer  the  goal  they  Vi-ished  to  reach.  Nor 
were  these  coimsels  given  to  a  small  and  weak 
party,  which  by  resistmg  might  bring  destruction 
upon  itself :  they  were  addi-essed  to  a  body  now 
approximating  in  numbers  half  the  population  of 
France.  They  were  given  to  a  body  which  had  in 
its  ranks  men  of  wealth,  nobles,  and  even  princes 
of  the  blood  :  a  body  that  could  raise  soldiers,  lead 
armies,  fight  battles,  and  win  victories.  Well, 
but,  says  CalTOi,  the  ^â€˘ictcries  of  the  battle-field 
are  barren ;  those  of  the  martyr  are  always  fruitful. 
One  of  the  latter  is  worth  a  score  of  the  former. 

Two  letters  haxo  been  forged  with  intent  to 
convict  the  Reformer  of  ha^^ng  prompted  to  the 
violent  courses  which  some  fiery  spirits  among  the 
French  Protestants  were  now  beginning  to  piu-sue.

archives  of  the  fiimily  of  D'Alisac,  but  their 
sjiiu-ioiisness  has  been  abmidantly  proved.-     They

Attempts  have  also  been  made  to  coimect  the 
Reformer  with  the  raid  of  the  notorious  Baron  des 
Ardrets.  This  man  signalised  his  short  career  as 
a  Protestant  by  invading  the  district  of  Lyons, 
slaughtering  Romanists,  sacking  churches,  making 
booty  of  the  priestly  vestments  and  the  sacred 
vessels,  and  appropriating  some  of  the  cathedrals 
for  the  Protestant  worship.  Did  Calvin  account 
these  acquisitions  a  gain  to  Protestantism  ?  Better, 
he  said,  worship  in  the  open  air,  in  dens  of  the 
earth,  anywhere,  than  in  edifices  so  accpiii'ed.  He 
wi-ote  to  Arcbets,  sharply  reproving  him,  and  con- 
demning the  outrages  by  which  he  had  disgraced 
the  holy  caxise,  for  the  sake  of  which  he  professed  to 
have  wrought  them.  A  similar  judgment  did  the 
Reformer  pronounce  on  the  conspiracy  of  Amboise, 
that  ill-omened  commencement  of  political  Pi'o- 
testantism  in  France.  "  Better,"  he  said,  writing 
to  the  heatl  of  that  conspiracy,  La  Renaudie, 
"  Better  we  should  all  perish  a  hundred  times  than 
be  the  cause  of  exposing  the  Gospel  to  such  a 
disgrace."" 

But  day  and  night  he  was  intent  on  marshalling 
the  spiritual  host,  and  leading  it  to  the  combat. 
Evangelists,  martyi's,  Clmrches  :  these  were  the 
three  arms â€” to  iise  ii  military  phrase â€” with  which 
he  carried  on  the  war.  Of  the  skill  and  pains 
which  he  devoted  to  the  preparation  of  the  latter 
weapon â€” the  organisation  of  Churches â€” we  give 
but  one  example. 

For  forty  years  the  evangelisation  of  France  had 
been  going  on.  There  were  now  small  congrega- 
tions in  several  of  its  towns.  In  May,  1559,  eleven 
ministers  assembled  in  Paris,  and  constituted  them- 
selves into  a  National  Synod.  This  affair  wUl  come 
before  us  more  fuUy  aftei'wards ;  we  notice  it 
here  as  necessary  to  the  complete  view  of  the 
work  of  Calvin.  His  plastic  hand  it  was  that 
communicated    to    the    Fi'ench    Protestants    that 


'  Bungener,  p.  302. 


=  lUd.,  pp.  304,  305. 


organisation  which  we  see  assumed  at  first  by  a 
mere  handful  of  pastors,  but  which  was  found  to 
be  equally  adapted  to  that  mighty  Church  of  thou- 
sands of  congregations  which,  ten  years  thereafter, 
was  seen  covering  the  soil  of  France. 

First  came  a  Confession  of  Faitli.  This  was  the 
basis  on  which  the  Church  was  to  stand,  the  root 
which  was  to  sustain  her  life  and  growth. 

Next  came  a  scheme  of  discipline.  This  wa.s 
meant  to  develop  and  conserve  that  new  life  which 
ouglit  to  spring  from  the  doctrines  confessed.  Mo- 
ralityâ€” in  other  word.s,  holiness â€” was  in  Calvin's 
oijinion  the  one  thing  essential  in  Churches. 

Lastly  came  a  gradiuited  machinery  of  courts, 
for  applying  that  discipline  or  government,  in  order 
to  the  conservation  and  development  of  that  mo- 
rality which  the  Reformer  judged  to  be  the  only 
result  of  any  value.  This  machinery  was  as 
follows  : â€” 

There  was  first  the  single  congregation,  or  Church 
of  the  locality,  with  its  pastor  and  small  staff  of 
associated  riders.  This  was  the  foundation.  Over 
the  Church  of  a  locality  were  placed  the  Churches 
of  the  district.  Each  congi-egation  sent  its  pastor 
and  an  elder  to  form  this  court,  which  w;is  termed 
the  Colloquy.  Over  the  Colloquy  were  the  Churches 
of  the  province,  termed  the  Conference;  and  over 
the  Conference  were  the  Churches  of  all  France,  or 
National  Synod. 

Tliis  constitution  was  essentially  democratic.  The 
whole  body  of  the  people â€” that  is,  the  members  of 
the  Church â€” were  the  primary  depositaries  of  this 
power ;  but  its  exercise  was  narrowed  at  each 
.  gradation  upwards.  It  began  with  the  local  con- 
gi-egation, which,  through  their  pixstor  and  elders, 
decided  on  all  matters  appertaining  to  themselves. 
Thence  it  passed  to  the  Colloquy,  which  adjudicated 
on  general  questions,  and  on  cases  of  appeal.  It 
proceeded  upwards  through  the  Provincial  Con- 
ference to  the  National  Sjniod,  which  was  the  most 
select  body  of  all,  being  constituted  of  two  pastors 
and  two  elders  from  each  province.  The  National 
Synod  passed  sentence  in  the  last  resort,  and  from 
its  decision  there  was  of  course  no  appeal. 

If  the  basis  of  this  government  was  broad,  being 
composed  of  the  whole  body  of  the  peoijle,  it  had 
for  its  apex  the  very  elite  of  the  clergy  and  laity. 
Liberty  was  secured,  but  so  too  were  order,  vigour, 
and  justice.  For  the  decision  of  the  most  important 
questions  it  reserved  the  highest  talents  and  the 
maturest  msdom.  It  combined  the  advantages  of 
a  democracy  with  those  of  a  monarchy.  Its  founda- 
tions were  as  mde  and  popular  as  the  constitution 
of  England,  but  counterpoised  by  the  weight  and 
influence    of    the    National    Synod,    even    as    the 


358 

government   of    England   is   by   tlie    dignity   and 
power  of  the  Crown. 

Calvin  did  not  carry  his  narrowing  process  the 
length  of  a  single  overseer  or  bishop.  Not  that 
he  held  it  unlawful  to  place  over  the  Church  a 
chief  pastor,  or  that  he  believed  that  the  Bible 
condemned  the  office  of  bishop  in  itself.  He  recom- 
mended an  episcopate  to  the  Church  of  Poland  :  ' 
he  allowed  the  office  of  bishop  in  the  Church  of 
England  r  and  he  has  so  expressed  himself  m  his 
Institutes,  as  to  leave  the  Church  at  liberty  on 
this  nead.  But  he  thought  he  could  more  clearly 
trace  in  the  New  Testament  such  a  distribution  of 
power  as  that  which  he  had  now  made,  and,  at  all 
events,  this  ecpiality  of  office  he  deemed  much  safer 
at  present  for  the  Church  of  France,  for  which  he 
foresaw  a  long  periotl  of  struggles  and  martyrdoms. 
He  would  not  expose  that  Church  to  seduction  by 
opening  to  her  ministers  the  path  of  official  or 
personal  aggrandisement.  The  fewer  the  dignities 
and  gi-andeurs  with  which  they  were  encompassed, 
the  more  easy  would  they  find  it  to  mount  the 
scaffold  ;    and  it  was  martyrs,  not  mitred  chiefs, 


HISTORY   OF   PROTESTANTISM. 


that  were  destined,  he  believed,  to  lead  the  Church 
to  victory. 

The  organisation  of  the  Church  of  France  Ijrought 
with  it  a  new  era  to  Protestantism  in  that  kingdom. 
From  this  time  forward  its  progi-ess  was  amazingly 
rapid.  Nobles  and  burgesses,  cities,  and  whole 
provmces  pressed  forward  to  join  its  ranks.  Con- 
gregations sprang  up  in  hundreds,  and  adherents 
flocked  to  them  in  tens  of  thousands.  The  entire 
nation  bade  foir  soon  to  terminate  its  divisions  and 
strifes  in  a  common  profession  of  the  Protestant 
faith.  Such  was  the  spectacle  that  cheered  the  last 
years  of  Calvin.  Wliat  a  profound  thankfulness â€” 
we  do  not  say  pride,  for  pride  he  banished  as  sinful 
in  coimection  with  such  a  cause â€” must  have  tilled 
the  bosom  of  the  Reformer,  when  he  reflected  that 
not  only  was  the  little  city  of  Geneva,  which  he 
had  won  for  tlie  Gospel  in  order  that  through  it  he 
might  win  mightier  realms,  preserved  from  over- 
throw in  the  midst  of  hostile  powers,  but  that  it 
had  become  the  centre  of  a  spiritual  empire  whose 
limits  would  far  exceed,  and  whose  duration  would 
long  out-last,  the  empire  of  Charles  ! 


CHAPTER    XXVII. 


THE     ACADEMY    OF     CiEXEVA. 


FouncTatiou  of  the  Academyâ€” Subscriptionsâ€” Its  Opening- Its  Literary  Eq  ipmentâ€” Its  Subsequent  Kenownâ€” Its 
Libraryâ€” Wliat  it  Suggests- Calvin's  Simplicity  oÂŁ  Lifeâ€” Sadoleto  Visits  liimâ€” The  Cardinal's  Surpriseâ€” Calvin's 
Povertyâ€” His  Charityâ€” He  Declines  the  Aid  of  tlie  Council. 


In  the  wake  of  the  Gospel,  learning  and  the  arts, 
Calvin  held,  should  ever  be  found.  Geneva  had 
become,  in  the  first  place,  a  fountain  of  Divine 
knowledge  to  the  suri'ounding  countries  ;  he  would 
make  it,  in  the  second  place,  a  fountain  of  science 
and  civilisation.  In  Italy,  letters  came  first ;  but 
in  England,  in  Bohemia,  in  Germany,  and  now  in 
Geneva,  the  Divine  science  opened  the  way,  and 
letters  and  phOosophy  followed.  It  was  drawing 
towards  the  evening  of  his  life,  when  Calvin  laid 
the  foundations  of  the  Academy  of  Geneva.  Next 
to  the  Reformation,  this  school  was  the  gi-eatest 
boon  that  he  conferred  on  the  republic  which  h;,d 
only  lately  em-olled  his  name  among  its  citizens. 
It  continued  lonff  after  he  was  dead  to  send  fortli 


distinguished  scholars,  in  every  department  of 
science,  and  to  shed  a  glory  on  the  little  State  in 
Avhich  it  was  planted,'  and  where  previous  to  the 
Reformation  scarcely  one  distinguished  man  was 
known. 

The  idea  of  such  an  institution  had  long  been 
before  the  mind  of  Calvin,  and  he  wished  not  to  die 
till  he  had  realised  it.  Having  communicated  his 
design  to  the  Council,  it  was  approved  of  by  their 
Excellencies,  and  in  1552  a  piece  of  ground  was 
purchased  on  which  to  erect  the  necessary  buildings. 
But  money  was  lacking.  Geneva  was  then  a  State 
of  but  from  15,000  to  20,000  inliabitants.  Its 
burdens  were  numerous.  It  had  to  exercise  hos- 
pitality to  from  one  to  two  tliousand  refugees.     It

had  to  endure  tlie  exjjeuses  of  war  in  a  time  of

had  waited  long  enough  on  the  Council,  Calvin 
now  set  on  foot  a  jiublic  subscription,  and  soon  he 
found  himself  in  possession  of  10,000  florins.  This 
was  little  for  the  object,  but  much  for  the  times. 
He  immediately  laid  the  foundations  of  the  edifice. 
He  marked  ^vith  joy  the  rising  walls;  tearing  him- 
self from  his  studies,  he  would  descend  from  the 
Hue  des  Chanoines  to  the  .scene  of  operations,  and 
tliough  enfeebled  by  quartan-ague  he  might  be  seen 
dragging  himself  over  the  works,  speaking  kindly 
words  now  and  again  to  the  workmen,  and  stimu  â–  
latmg  them  by  expressing  his  satisfictiou  at  their 
progi-ess. 

Two  edifices  were  rLsing  at  the  same  moment 
under  the  eye  of  the  Eeformer.  The  organisation

appointed  rector;  five  mastenshipsâ€” Calvin  had 
asked  seven â€” one  of  Hebrew,  one  of  Greek,  one  of

In  1565,  a  year  after  the  death  of  the  Eeformer, 
there  was  added  a  lecture.ship  in  law.  With  her 
Academyâ€” which,  however,  was  but  the  top-stone

ever  for  the  great  spiritual  and  moral  sovereignty 
wliich  Calvin  intended  that  she  should  exercise  in 
Europe.^

The  position  which  Calvin  now  filled  was  one  of 
gi-eater  influence  than  perhaps  any  one  man  had 
exercised  in  the  Church  of  Christ  since  the  days  of 
the  apostles.     He  was  the  counsellor  of  kings ;  he 
was  the  adviser  of  j)rinc6S  and  statesmen ;  he  cor- 
responded with  warriors,  scholars,  and  Reformers; 
he  consoled  martyrs,  and  organised  Churches  ;  his 
admonitions  were  submitted  to,  and  his  letters  trea- 
sured, as  mai-ks  of  no  ordinary  distinction.      All

influence,    was    in    Ufe    and    mannei-s    in     nowise 
diflerent  from  an  ordinary  citizen  of  Geneva.     He 
was  as  humbly  lodged,  he  was  as  simply  clothed, 
and  he  was  served   by  as   few  attendants  as   any 
burgess  of  them  all.      He  had  been  poor  all  his 
days,  and  he  continued  so  to  the  end.    One  day  a 
cardinal  of  the  Roman  Church,  Sadoleto,  who  hap- 
pened to  be  p.ossing  through  Geneva,  would  pay 
him   the  honour  of   a  visit.       He  was  conducted 
to  No.   122,  Rue  des  Chanoines,  and  told  to  his 
sui-prise  that  this  was  the  house  of  the  Reformer. 
A  yet  gi-eater  surprise  awaited  the  cardinal.      He 
knocked   for  entrance :    there  was  no  porter  at  the 
gate  ;  no  seiTant  in  livery  gave  him  admission  :  it 
was  Calvin  himself  that  opened  the  door.

CHAPTER  XXYIII. 


THE   SOCIAL   AND    FAMILY    LIFE    OF    GENEVA. 

The  Daily.  Sermonâ€” Its  Attractivenessâ€” Daily  Life  of  the  Citizenâ€” His  Dressâ€” His  Tableâ€” Development  of  "Wealthâ€” 
The  Refugees â€” The  Benefits  they  conferred  on  their  Adopted  Country â€” English  Names  on  the  Genevan  Registers 
â€”The  Sabhath  in  Geneva; 


Now  that  Calvin  lias  realised  liis  programme,  let  us 
look  at  the  social  and  family  life  of  the  Genevese. 
The  'â€˘â–   Christian  Idea,"  as  Gaberel  calls  it,  had 
created  their  State,  and  religion  was  the  all-per- 
vading and  dominant  element  in  it.  Calvin,  the 
j)cople,  the  State â€” all  thi-ee  were  one,  the  fusion 
was  complete,  and  the  policy  of  the  Senate,  and 
the  action  of  the  citizens,  were  but  the  results  of 
that  great  principle  which  had  called  into  existence 
this  marvellous  community.  The  "  Sermon  "  held 
a  first  place  among  their  institutions.  Day  by 
day  it  reiiivigorated  that  spirit  which  was  the 
"  breatli "  of  Geneva.  But,  besides  the  need  the 
Genevans  felt  of  the  instructions  and  consolations 
of  religion,  there  were  other  influences  that  acted 
in  drawing  them  to  the  temples.  Preaching  was 
then  a  novelty.  Like  break  of  day  in  an  Eastern 
clime,  the  Gospel,  in  mid-day  effulgence,  had  all  at 
once  burst  on  these  men  after  the  darkness  of  the 
j\liddle  Ages.  Scarce  had  the  first  faint  silvery 
streaks  shown  themselves,  when  lo !  the  full 
flood  of  the  sun's  light  was  poured  upon  them. 
The  same  generation  which  had  listened  to  the 
monks,  had  now  the  privilege  of  listening  to  the 
Reformers.  From  tales,  legends,  and  miracles, 
which  were  associated  in  their  minds  with  the  yoke 
of  foreign  masters,  they  passed  to  the  pure  and 
elevating  doctrines  of  the  Word  of  God,  which, 
apart  from  their  own  beauty  and  majesty,  were, 
they  knew,  the  source  whence  had  come  their  jwli- 
tical  and  civil  independenca  We  at  this  day  can 
but  ftxintly  realise  the  charm  that  must  then  have 
hung  round  the  pulpit,  and  which  assembled,  day 
after  day,  the  Genevese  in  crowds,  to  the  preaching 
of  the  Gospel. 

At  Geneva,  the  magistrate  as  well  as  the  artisan 
invariably  began  the  day  with  an  act  of  worship. 
At  six  in  the  morning  the  churches  were  opened, 
and  crowds  might  be  seen  in  every  quarter  of 
the  city  on  their  way  to  spend  an  hour  in  listen- 
ing to  the  "Exposition."  After  this  the  youth 
assembled  in  school  or  college,  and  the  father  and 
the  elder  sons  repaired  to  the  workshops.  The 
mid-day  repast,  which  was  taken  in  common  with 
the  domestics,  again  re-united  the  family.     After 


dianer  the  head  of  the  household  paid  a  shoi-t  visit 
to  his  club '  to  hear  the  news.  And  what  were  the 
events  on  which  the  Genevan  kept  his  eyes  intently 
fixed,  and  for  wluch  he  waited  from  day  to  day 
with  no  ordinary  airxiety  to  receive  tidiiigs  ?  The 
great  drama  in  j^rogi-ess  around  him  completely 
occupied  his  thoughts.  How  goes  the  battle,  he 
would  ask,  between  Protestantism  and  Rome  in 
France,  in  Italy,  in  Spain  1  Has  any  fresh  edict  of 
ljei"secution  issued  these  days  past  from  the  Vatican? 
Has  any  one  been  called  to  yield  up  his  life  on  the 
scaffold,  and  what  were  his  last  words  1  AVhat 
number  of  refugees  have  anived  in  our  city  since 
yesterday,  and  through  what  perils  and  sufferings 
have  they  managed  to  reach  our  gates'?  Such 
were  the  topics  that  furnished  matter  of  daily  talk 
to  the  Genevese.  The  narrow  limits  of  their  little 
State  were  far  from  forming  their  horizon.  Their 
thoughts  and  sjanpathies  were  as  extensive  as 
Christendom.  Tliere  was  not  a  prisoner,  not  a 
martyr  for  the  Gosjiel  in  any  of  its  countries  for 
whom  they  did  not  feel  and  pray ;  he  was  their 
brother.  Not  a  reverse  befell  the  cause  of  the 
Eefonn  in  any  part  of  the  field  which  they  did  not 
mourn,  nor  a  success  in  which  they  did  not  rejoice. 
They  were  watching  a  battle  which  would  bring 
triumph  or  overthrow,  not  to  Geneva  only,  but  to 
the  Gospel ;  hence  the  gi-avity  and  greatness  of 
their  characters.  "  The  Genevan  of  that  day,"  says 
Gaberel,  "  took  the  same  interest  in  the  news  of  the 
kingdom  of  God,  which  he  takes  to-day  in  the  dis- 
cussion of  material  afiixii-s."^ 

The  fixmily  life  of  the  Genevans  at  that  period 
was  characterised  by  severe  simplicity.  Theii-  dress 
was  wholly  -Nvithout  ornament.  The  magistrates 
wore  cloth  ;  the  ordinary  Inirgess  contented  himself 
with  serge.  Tliis  difference  in  their  attii'e  was  not 
held  as  marking  any  distinction  of  class  among  the 
citizens,  for  the  members  of  the  Councils  were 
cliosen  entu-ely  with  reference  to  their  merit,  and 
in  nowise  from  any  considei-ation  of  birtli  or 
wealth.     Nor  did  this  avoidance   of  superfluities

lead  to  any  falling  off  in  the  industrial  activity  or 
the  inventive  skill  of  the  citizens.  On  the  contrary, 
the  arts  and  industries  flourished,  and  both  the 
citizens  of  Geneva,  and  the  refugees  who  found 
asylum  within  it,  became  famous  for  their  manu- 
facture of  objects  of  utility  and  luxury,  which  they 
exported  to  other  countries. 

If  their  dress  was  marked  by  plainness,  not  less 
were  their  tables  by  frugality.  The  rich  and  poor 
alike  were  obliged  to  obey  the  simiptuary  laws. 
"  The  heads  of  families,"  says  Gaberel,  "  seeing  the 
ease,  the  health,  the  good  order,  the  morality  that 
now  reigned  in  theii-  dwellings,  blessed  those 
rigorous  laws,  which  only  goui-mands  fomid  tyran- 
nical, who  remembered  with  regi-et  the  full  tables 
of  other  days."'  We  dare  say  some  of  these  men 
would  have  wished  rather  that  theii'  dinners  had 
been  ampler,  though  their  liberty  had  been  less. 
They  are  not  the  first  who  have  thought  the  bless- 
ing of  freedom  too  dearly  purchased  with  the 
sacrifice  of  damti&s. 

When  j)eriods  of  distress  came  round,  occasioned 
by  war  or  famine,  the  citizens  were  especially  sen- 
sible of  the  benefit  of  tliis  simple  and  frugal  manner 
of  life.  They  felt  less  the  privations  they  had  thou 
to  bear,  and  were  able  to  support  with  dignity  the 
misfoi-tunes  of  the  State.  Moreover,  as  the  result 
of  this  economy,  the  wealth  of  the  citizens  was 
rapidly  developed,  and  the  State  reached  a  pros- 
perity it  had  never  known  in  former  days.  Each 
citizen  laid  by  religiously  a  certain  portion  of  his 
earnings,  and  the  years  of  gi-eatest  calamity  were 
precisely  those  that  were  signalised  by  the  greatest 
beneficence.  Instead  of  recei\-iug  support  from 
other  States,  Geneva  .sent  its  charities  to  the 
coimtries  around,  becoming  a  storehouse  of  earthly 
as  of  heavenly  bread  to  the  nations.  These 
citizens,  who  wore  plain  blouses,  and  sat  down  to 
a  meal  correspondingly  plain,  entertained  during 
many  years,  ^vith  liberal  Christian  hospitality,  the 
refugees  of  religionâ€” nobles,  scholars,  statesmen, 
and  men  of  bu-th.  The  Genevan  citizen,  inde- 
pendent in  means,  and  adding  thereto  that  mental 
independence  which  the  Gospel  gives,  could  not 
but  be  a  being  of  conscious  dignity,  and  of  cha- 
racter inherently  gi-and,  whom  no  call  of  devotion 
or  heroism  would  find  miprepared. 

Geneva  profited  immensely  in  another  way  by 
the  movement,  of  which  it  had  become  the  head- 
quartera.  The  men  who  crowded  to  it,  and  to 
whom  it  so  hospitably  opened  its  gates,  conferred 
on  it  gi-eater  advantages  than  any  they  received 
from  it.     They  were  of  every  rank,  profession,  and 


'  Gtaberel,  torn,  i.,  p.  390. 


trade,  and  they  brought  to  the  city  of  their  adop- 
tion, not  refinement  of  birth  and  elegance  of  letters 
only,  but  also  new  arts  and  improved  industries. 
There  immediately  ensued  a  great  quickening  of 
the  energies  of  labour  and  skill  in  Geneva,  and 
these  brought  in  theii-  turn  that  wealth  and  con- 
scious dignity  which  labour  and  .skill  never  faU  to 
impart.  It  is  a  new  nation  that  we  behold  form- 
ing on  the  soil  of  the  republic,  mth  germs  and 
elements  in  its  bosom,  higher  and  more  various 
than  infant  State  had  ever  before  enjoyed.  The 
fathei-s  of  the  great  Eoman  people  were  but  a 
band  of  outlaws  and  adventurers !  How  dif- 
ferent the  men  we  now  see  assembling  on  the 
shores  of  the  Leman  to  lay  the  foundations  of  the 
Rome  of  Protestantism,  from  those  who  had 
gathered  at  the  foot  of  the  Capitoline  to  lay  the 
first  stone  in  the  Eternal  City !  From  the  strand 
of  Naples  to  the  distant  shores  of  Scotland,  we 
behold  Protestantism  weeding  out  of  the  sur- 
roimding  countries,  and  a.ssembling  at  this  great 
focus,  all  who  were  skilful  in  art,  as  well  as  illus- 
trious in  \'ii-tue,  and  they  communicated  to  Geneva 
a  refinement  of  mamiei-s  and  an  ai-tistic  skill  which 
it  continues  to  retain  after  the  lapse  of  thi-ee 
centuries. 

The  most  important  question  raised  by  the 
arrival  of  these  exiles  was  not.  Where  shall  bread 
be  found  for  them  1  The  hospitality  of  the  Genevese 
solved  this  difficulty,  for  scarce  was  there  citizen 
who  had  not  one  or  more  of  these  strangers  living 
under  his  i-oof,  and  sitting  at  his  table.  The  ques- 
tion which  the  Genevese  had  most  at  heart  was, 
How  shall  we  utilise  this  great  access  of  intel- 
lectual, moral,  and  industrial  power  1  How  shall 
we  draw  forth  the  varied  capabilities  of  these  men 
in  the  way  of  strengthening,  enriching,  and 
glorifying  the  State?  Let  us  begin,  said  they, 
by  em'olling  them  as  citizens.  "  But,"  said  the 
Libertines,  when  the  proposal  was  firet  mooted, 
"  is  it  fail-  that  new-comers  should  lay  down  the 
law  to  the  children  of  the  land  1  These  men 
were  not  born  on  the  soil  of  the  reiiublic." 

True,  it  was  answered,  but  then  the  republic 
is  not  an  aflfair  of  acres,  it  is  an  affair  of  faith. 
The  true  Geneva  is  Protestantism,  and  these  men 
were  born  into  the  State  in  the  same  hour  in 
which  they  became  Protestants.  Tliis  broad  view 
of  the  question  prevailed.  Nevertheless,  the 
honour  was  sparingly  distributed.  Up  till  1555, 
only  eighty  had  received  the  freedom  of  the  city; 
in  the  early  pai-t  of  that  year,  other  sixty 
were   added' â€” a   small    number    truly  when    we 

-  Bungener,  p.  226.

HISTORY  OF  PROTESTANTISM. 


think  how  numeiwis  the  Protestant  exiles  were. 
The  greatest  of  all  the  sons  of  Geneva,  he  who 
was  more  than  a  citizea,  who  was  the  founder  of 
the  State,  was  not  legally  enrolled  till  five  yeai-s 
before  his  death.  The  name  of  John  Knox  was 
earlier  inscribed  on  the  Registers  than  that  of  John 
Calvin.  Hardly  was  there  a  country  in  Europo 
which  did  not  help  to  swell  this  truly  Catholic  roll. 
The  list  contributed  by  Italy  alone  was  a  long  and' 
brilliant  one.  Lucca  sent,  among  other  distin- 
guished names,  the  Calendrini,  the  Burlamachi,  the 
Turretini,  and  the  Micheli.'  Of  these  families  many 
took  root  in  Geneva,  and  by  the  services  which 
they  rendered  the  State,  and  the  splendour  their 
genius  shed  upon  it  in  after-days,  they  repaid  a 
himdred-fold  as  citizens  the  welcome  they  had 
received  as  refugees.  Othei-s  returned  to  theii' 
native  land  when  pereecution  had  abated.  "  When 
the  English  returned,"  says  Misson,  "  they  left  in 
the  Register,  which  is  still  preserved,  a  list  of  their 
names  and  qualities â€” Stanley,  Spencer,  Musgrave, 
Pelham,  are  among  the  first  ill  it,  as  they  ought 
to  be.  The  title  of  citizen,  which  several  had 
obtained,  was  continued  to  them  by  an  order,  and 
compliment  of  the  Seigniory,  so  that  several  earls 
and  peers  of  England  may  as  well  boast  of  being 
citizens  of  Geneva  as  Paul  did  of  being  ft  citizen 
of  Rome." 

One  of  the  most  striking  chai-acteristics  of  the 
Geneva  of  that  day,  and  for  a  century  after,  espe- 
cially to  one  coming  from  a  Popish  country,  was  its 
Sabbath.  The  day  brought  a  complete  cessation 
of  labour  to  all  classes  :  the  field  was  unwatered 
by  the  swea,t  of  the  husbandman,  the  air  was  un- 
vexed  by  the  hammer  of  the  artisan,  and  the  lake 
was  unploughed  by  the  keel  of  the  fisherman.  The 
gi-eat  bell  of  St.  Peter's  has  sounded  out  its  sum- 
mons, the  citizens  have  assembled  in  the  churches, 
the  city  gates  have  been  closed,  and  no  one  is 
allowed  to  enter  or  depart  while  the  citizens  are 
occupied  in  offering  then-  worship. 

Everywhere  the  stillness  of  the  sacred  day  is 
sublime,  but  here  that  sublimity  was  enhanced  by 
the  grandeur  of  the  region.  The  Sabbath  seemed 
to  shed  its  owii  jiure  and  peaceful  splendour  upon 
the  sublimities  of  nature,  and  these  sublimities,  in 
their  timi,  seemed  to  impart  an  additional  sanctity 
and  majesty  to  the  Sabbath.  Thei-e  was  peace  on 
the  blue  waveless  Lemau ;  there  was  peace  on 
those  plains  that  enclosed  it  in  their  vast  sweep, 

'  Misson,  vol.  ii.,  part  1,  p.  275.  Besides  the  names

and  on  whoso  bosom  the  chMet  lay  hid  amid 
festooned  vines  and  tall  pine-ti-ees.  There  was 
peace  on  the  green  i-ampart  of  tlie  Jura,  and  peace 
on  the  distant  Alps,  v.'hich  in  the  opposite  quarter 
of  the  horizon  lift  their  snowy  piles  into  the  sky, 
and  stand  sUent  and  solemn  as  worshippei-s.  A 
su])erb  temple,  indeed,  seems  the  region,  walled 
in  by  natural  grandeure,  and  pervaded  through- 
out with  a  Sabbatic  peace.  In  the  midst  of  it  is 
the  little  city  of  Geneva.  No  stirs  or  tumults  are 
heard  within  it ;  its  lÂ«lls  and  its  psalms  only 
salute  the  ear.  Beaming  faces,  the  sign  of  happy 
hearts,  tell  what  a  day  of  gladness  it  is â€” the  most 
gladsome  of  all  the  seven.  In  eveiy  dwelling  is 
heard  "  the  melody  of  health."  But  we  must  go 
to  St.  Peter's,  would  we  see  in  its  highest  mani- 
fe.station  the  power  of  the  Sabbath  to  raise 
the  souls  and  mould  the  charactei-s  of  a  people. 
A  crowd  of  magnanimous,  earnest,  intelligent 
faces  look  up  aroimd  the  pulpit.  There  are 
gathered  the  finest  intellects  and  holiest  spiiits  of 
all  Christendom,  for  whatever  was  noble  and  pure 
in  other  countries  had  been  chased  thither.  The 
worship  of  men  like  these  could  be  no  common 
affaii',  no  mere  show  or  pantomime,  like  that  per- 
formed in  bespangled  vestments  amid  lighted  tapei-s. 
The  worshippers  in  St.  Peter's  were  men  whose  souls 
had  been  attempered  in  the  fire,  and  who,  having 
forsaken  all  worldly  goods  for  the  sake  of  the 
Gospel,  stood  prepared  every  hour  to  sacrifice  life 
itself.  Their  worship  was  the  worship  of  the  heart, 
and  their  praj^er  the  prayer  of  faith  that  ])ierces 
the  heavens. 

And  as  the  devotion  of  the  hearers  was  entire, 
so  the  instructions  of  the  pulpit  were  lofty.  The 
jireacher  might  not  be  always  eloquent,  but  he  was 
never  tame.  He  forgot  himself  and  remembered 
only  his  gi'eat  theme.  Did  he  discoui-se  on  some 
2X)iiit  of  doctrine,  his  exposition  was  clear,  his  words 
weighty ;  did  he  plead  the  cause  of  the  confessors 
of  other  lands,  "  led  as  sheep  to  the  slaughter,"  it 
was  with  a  truthfulness  and  pathos  that  made  his 
hearers  mingle  their  tears  with  his,  and  prejiared 
them  to  open  their  doora  to  such  of  the  pei-secuted 
as  might  escape  the  prisons  and  stakes  which  theii' 
enemies  had  prepared  for  them.  Such  were  the 
scenes  that  might  be  witnessed  every  Sabbath  in 
those  days  within  the  walls  of  St.  Peter's,  Geneva. 
If  Geneva  was  the  "inner  Biu-eau"  of  the  European 
Reformation,  as  Gaberel  says,  the  pulpit  Wiis  the 
inner  spring  of  power  in  that  "  Bureau."  While 
the  pulpit  of  Geneva  stood,  Geneva  would  stand ; 
if  the  j)ulpit  shoidd  fiill,  Geneva  too  would  fall.  It 
was  the  bulwark  of  its  liberties,  the  "  horses  and 
chariots "   that   guarded   the    independence    of  the

CHAPTER  XXIX. 

Calvin's  last   illxess  and   death.

To  the  Reformer  the  close  was  now  near.    His  body, 
never  robust,  had  become  latterly  the  seat  of  nume- 
rous maladies,  that  made  life  a  prolonged  torture. 
The  quartan-ague  of  1559  he  had  never  recovered 
from.     He  was  afflicted  with  pains  in  his  head, 
and  pains  in  his  limbs.     Food  was  often  nauseous 
to  him.     He  suffered  from  asthma,  and  sjntting  of 
blood.     He  had  to  sustain  the  attacks  of  the  gout, 
and^  the  yet  more  excruciating  agony  of  the  stone. 
Amid  the  ruins  of  his  body,  his  spirit  was  fresh,  and 
clear,  and  vigorous  as  ever;    but  as  the  traveller 
quickens  his  steps  when  the  evening  begins  to  fall, 
and  the  shadows  to  lengthen,  Calvin  redoubled  hijs 
efforts,  if  so,  before  breathing  his  last,  he  might 
make  that  legacy  of  wsdom  and  truth  he  was  to 
leave  to  the  Church  still  more  complete  and  per- 
fect.    His  friends  in  many  lands  wrote  imploring 
him^  to  take  a  little  rest.     Calvin  saw  restâ€” evei- 
lasting  restâ€” coming  mth  the  deepenmg  shadows, 
and   contiaiued  to  work   on.     Beza   tells  us   that 
durmg  his  last  malady  he  translated  from  L;itiii 
into  French  his  Harmony  on  Moses,  revised   the 
translation   of  Genesis,  wote  upon   the   Book  of 
Joshua,   and   finally   revised    and    corrected    the 
gi-eater   jm-t    of   his    annotations    on    the    New 
Testament.      He  was  all  the  while  receiving  and 
answering  letters  from  the  Churches.      He  had  but 
a  Httle  before  given  the  last  touches  to  his  im- 
mortal work,  the  Institutes. 

The  hist  time  he  appeared  in  the  pulpit  was  on 
the  6th  of  February,  1564.  ^  On  that  occasion  he 
was  seized  with  so  .-iolent  a  fit  of  coughing  that  it 
brought   the   blood   into  his  mouth,  and   stopped 


'  Kuchat,  torn,  vii.,  p.  41. 


his  utterance.  As  he  descended  the  stau-s,  amid 
the  breathless  stillness  of  hLs  flock,  all  understood 
but  too  well  that  his  last  words  in  the  pulpit  of 
St.  Peter's  had  been  spoken.  There  followed  weeks 
of  intense  suiTering.  To  the  martyr  when  mount- 
ing the  scaffold  the  Reformer  had  said,  "Be  strong, 
and  play  the  man:"  during  four  months  of 
suffering,  not  less  severe  than  that  of  the  scaffold, 
w;is  Calvin  to  display  the  heroism  which  he  had 
preached  to  others.  The  more  violent  attacks  of 
his  malady  were  indicated  only  by  the  greater 
pallor  of  his  face,  the  quivering  of  his  lips,  the 
tremulous  motion  of  his  clasi^ed  hands,  and  the 
half-suppressed  ejaculation,  "  O  Lord  !  how  lon^  ?" 
It  was  during  these  months  of  suflering  that  he 
prosecuted  the  labours  of  v.diich  Beza,  who  wa.s 
daily  by  his  bedside,  tells  us  in  the  passage  referred 
to  above.  A  little  cold  water  was  often  his  only  â€˘ 
noiu-ishment  for  daj's,  and  haraig  refreshed  himseif 
therewith,  he  would  again  resume  work. 

On  this  death-bed  were  riveted  the  eyes  of  all 
Christendom.  Rome  waited  the  issue  of  his  sick- 
ness with  intense  excitement,  in  the  hope  that  it 
would  rid  her  of  her  great  foe.  The  Churches  of 
the  Reformation  masked  -svith  sorrowful  and  most 
aflectionate  anxiety  if  their  fiither  was  to  be  taken 
from  their  head.  MeanwhUe,  as  though  to  impress 
the  minds  of  men,  and  make  a  great  mourning 
around  this  mighty  bier,  the  plague  broke  in,  and 
inflicted  unprecedented  ravages  on  almost  all  the 
countries  of  Europe.  It  traversed  Germany,  France, 
and  Switzerland,  "and  men  fell  before  it,"  says 
Ruchat,  "  as  fall  the  leaves  in  autumn  when  the 
tempest  sweeps  through  the  forest."  This  pesti- 
lence was  equally  fatal  on  the  moimtaiu-top  and  in

to  his  great  weakness,  the  CouncO  resolved  to  visit 
bim  at  his  o^vn  house.  Accordingly,  on  the  30th 
April,  the  twenty-live  Lords  of  Geneva,  in  all  the 
l^omij  of  a  public  ceremony,  proceeded  to  his  humble 
dwelling  in  the  Rue  des  ChanoLnes.  Raising  him- 
self on  his  bed,  he  exhorted  them,  amongst  other 
thmgs,  to  maintain  ever  inviolate  the  indepen- 
dence of  a  city  which  God  had  destined  to  high 
ends.  But  he  reminded  them  that  it  was  the 
Gospel  which  alone  made  Geneva  worth  preserving, 
and  that  therefore  it  behoved  them  to  guard  its 
purity  if  they  would  preserve  for  then-  city  the 
protection  of  a  stronger  arm  than  their  own.  Com- 
mending them  and  Geneva  to  God,  and  begging 
them  one  and  all,  says  Beza,  to  pardon  him  his 
faults,  he  held  out  his  hand  to  them,  which  they 
gra-sped  for  the  last  time,  and  retired  as  from  the 
death-bed  of  a  father." 

On  the  morrow  he  received  the  pastors.  Most 
affectionate  and  touching  was  his  address.  He 
exhorted  them  to  diligence  in  their  office  as 
jweachers,  to  show  fidelity  to  the  flock,  to  cultivate 
affection  for  one  another,  and,  above  all,  maintain 
the  Reformation  and  discipline  which  he  had  esta- 
blished in  the  Church.  He  reminded  them  of  the 
conflict  he  had  had  to  wage  in  this  matter,  and  the 
afflictions  that  had  befallen  him,  and  how  at  length 
God  had  been  pleased  to  crown  his  labours  with 
success.  His  many  maladies  and  sicknesses,  he  .said, 
had  at  times  made  him  morose  and  hard  to  please, 
and  even  irascible.  For  these  failings  he  asked 
pardon,  first  of  God,  and  then  of  his  brethren ;  and, 
"  finally,"  Beza  adds,  "  he  gave  liis  hand  to  each, 
one  after  the  other,  which  was  with  such  anguish 
and  bittei-ness  of  heart  in  every  one,  that  I  cannot 
e'.'en  recall  it  to  miiul  ^vithout  extreme  sadness." 

The  Council  he  had  bidden  farewell,  his  brethren 
he  had  bidden  farewell,  but  there  was  one  friend, 
the  oldest  of  all  save  Cordier,  who  had  not  yet 
stood  at  his  death-bed  and  received  his  last  adieus. 
On  the  2nd  May,  Calvin  received  a  letter  from 
Farel,  in  which  the  writer  intimated  that  he  wa.s 
just  setting  out  to  visit  him.  Farel  was  now  nearly 
eighty.  Could  he  not  wait  the  little  while  till  he 
had  put  ofl'  "  this  t^ibernacle,"  and  then,  with  less 
difficulty  to  either,  the  two  friends  would  meet  1  So 
it  would  seem   did   Calvm   tliink,   and  hence  the

This  time  Farel  may  not  stop  him.-

Then  he  offered  prayer,  but  not  without  difficulty,

with  as  cheerful  a  face  as  he  could â€” '  A  partition

spoken  truly.  From  the  Ijed  to  which  he  had  been 
can-ied  he  was  to  rise  no  more.

While  that  empire  lives,  Cahin  will  live.

CALVIN  S   WORK.

When  the  tidings  sped  thi-ough  Europe  that  Calvin 
was  dead,  the  two  great  parties  into  which  Chris- 
tendom was  divided  were  very  difterently  affected. 
The  one  gave  way  to  unbounded  joy,  the  other  was 
seized  with  nearly  as  nnboiuided  sorrow.  Rome, 
heai-ing  in  the  news  the  knell  of  Protestantism, 
confidently  anticipated  the  immediate  i-eturn  of  the 
revolted  countries  to  their  obedience.  "  The  man  of 
Geneva,"  as  she  termed  the  Reformer,  was  no  more. 
Tlie  arm  which  had  so  often  smitten  her  legions, 
and  chased  them  from  the  field  in  disastrous  rout, 
would  never  again  be  lifted  up  in  battle ;  and  she 
had  nothing  more  to  do,  in  order  to  restore  her 
Church  to  its  former  glory  and  dominion,  than 
simply  to  go  forth  and  summon  the  Reforming 
ranks,  now  left  without  a  leader,  to  surrender. 
The  Pope  went  so  far  as  to  nominate  seven  com- 
missioners, who  were  to  proceed  to  Geneva  on  this 
business."  This  step  was  taken  with  the  advice, 
amongst  others,  of  Cardinal  Boromeo  and  the 
Bishoji  of  Anneci,  who  seem  to  have  persuaded  the 
Pope  that  the  CouncQ  and  citizens  of  Geneva  only 
waited  for  some  such  embassage  to  abandon  Protes-

If  Rome  thought,  on  tlie  one  hand,  tJiat  the

not  quite  fifty-five  j'eai-s,  had  embodied  all  the 
principles  of  the  movement  in  his  ^^Titings ;  he 
had  enshrined  them  as  in  a  living  model  in  Geneva, 
through  Geneva  he  had  initiated  the  gi'eat  work  of

The  eye  truly  enlightened  covdd  see  him  still

ruling  Christendom  from  it  as  from  a  throne. 
While  the  Reformation  was  there,  Calvin  was  there; 
and  if  at  Geneva,  it  was  in  France,  and  in  all 
Christendom.  Both  those  who  triumjjhed  and 
those  who  trembled,  thinking  the  last  hour  was 
about  to  strike  to  Geneva  and  the  Reformation, 
were  alike  mistaken.     The  city  rose  higher  than 


LUTHER'S   AND   CALVIN'S   REFORMATION.

befere,  tliougli  tlie  man  who  made  it  famous  was  in 
liis  grave.  The  movement  spread  wider  tlian  ever, 
and  if  the  city  was  a  centre  and  impelling  power 
to  the  movement,  the  movement  was  a  bulwark 
around  the  city.  "  The  Genevese  of  the  sixteenth 
century,"  says  an  eloquent  modem  writer,  "  com- 
mitted one  of  those  deeds  of  saintly  daring  which 
seem  folly  in  the  eyes  of  men,  but  which  are  in 
reality  the  safeguard  of  nations  heroic  enough  to 
attempt  them.  Geneva  had  been  the  representative 
of  a  gre^at  right,  liberty  of  conscience ;  she  offered 
an  asylum  to  all  the  martyi's  of  the  faith ;  she  had 
put  her  hand  to  the  work,  and  pursued  her  career 
without  casting  a  look  behind.  Politicians  and 
calculators  may,  if  they  please,  see  a  sort  of  mad- 
ness in  a  republic,  without  strength  or  riches, 
proclaiming  religious  and  moral  liberty  in  the  face 
of  Italy,  Spain,  and  France,  united  for  the  triumjjh 
of  Romish  despotism.  But  the  God  of  the  faithful 
ones  who  hold  fast  the  ti'uth  coufomided  human 
prevision,  he  surrounded  our  to^vn  'with  that  celestial 
protection,  against  which  the  plots  and  the  rage  of 
the  mighty  broke  in  vain.  Thus  Geneva,  without 
arms  and  without  territory,  accomplished  her 
perilous  mission ;  and  remaining  faithful  to  the 
principle  of  her  nationality,  tlie  city  of  Calvin  saw 
herself  the  object  of  the  Divine  favour,  and  enjoj-ed 
a  prosperity,  a  respect,  and  an  outward  security 
which  the  most  jxiwerful  States  in  the  world  do  not 
often  obtain."' 

Now  that  we  have  come  to  the  close  of  Calvin's 
career,  it  is  necessary  that  we  should  pause,  and 
ask  wherein  lay  his  distinctive  chai-acteristic  as  a 
Refoi-mei-,  and  what  was  it  that  constituted  the 
specific  difference  between  his  Reformation  and 
that  of  Luther.  The  answer  to  this  inquiry  will 
help  us  to  understand  the  unity  that  belongs  to 
the  great  di-ama  whose  successive  developments  we 
are  attempting  to  trace.  The  work  of  Luther  was 
needed  to  prepare  for  that  of  Calvin,  and  Calvin's 
was  necessary  to  complete  and  crown  that  of 
Luther.  The  pai-ts  which  each  acted  were  essential 
to  constitute  a  whole.  Wittemberg  and  Geneva 
make  between  them  one  Reformation.  This  can  be 
better  seen  in  o\u-  day  than  when  Luther  and 
Calvin  were  alive,  and  toiling  each  at  his  allotted 
part  of  the  great  ta.sk. 

Let  us  first  sketch  in  outline  the  difference 
between  theso  two  men  and  their  work,  and  then 
return  and  explain  it  a  little  more  in  det-ail. 

By  the  year  1535,  the  Reformation  in  Germany 
had   culmLnate<l,    and   was   begimiing   to    decUne. 


1  Gaberel,  torn,  i.,  p.  466;  1858â€”1862. 


The  Augsburg  Confession  (1530)  marked  the  era  of 
greatest  prosperity  to  German  Protestantism  ;  the 
formation  of  the  Schmalkald  League  notified  the 
moment  of  its  incipient  decline. 

That  League,  in  itself,  was  quite  defensible â€” nay, 
even  dutiful,  considering  the  power  of  the  princes, 
and  the  attempts  the  emperor  was  making  to 
destroy  the  political  system  of  Germany.  But  it 
exercised,  especially  after  the  death  of  Luther,  a 
depressmg  and  withering  efiect  upon  the  spiritual 
energies  of  the  Protestants,  which  did  more  to 
throw  back  the  movement  than  would  any  amount 
of  ^â– iolence  that  could  have  been  inflicted  upon  it. 
With  Luther  in  his  gi-ave,  with  jMelancthon  and  his 
compromises,  ^\ith  Landgrave  Philip  and  his  soldiers, 
the  Reformation  in  Germany  had  closed  its  period  of 
well-doing.  Another  centre  had  to  be  found  where 
the  movement  might  have  a  fresh  start.  Geneva 
was  selected.  There  the  Reformation  was  extricated 
from  the  political  entanglements  with  which  it  had 
liecome  mixed  up  in  Germany.  It  was  rescued 
from  the  hands  of  jxilitical  and  military  men  :  it 
was  withdrawn  from  reliance  on  armies,  and 
committed  to  those  who  could  further  it  only  with 
then-  prayers  and  their  martyrdoms.  Trae,  its 
second  cradle  was  placed  on  a  spot  which,  of  all 
others,  seemed  open  to  attack  on  every  side,  and 
where  it  was  not  sure  of  a  day's  life ;  yet  around 
that  spot  were  invisible  ramparts ;  the  poise  con- 
stantly mauitauie<;l  in  the  ambitions  of  its  neigh- 
bouring sovereigns â€” Charles,  Francis,  and  the  Pope 
â€” was  to  it  for  walls. 

As  a  new  foothold  had  to  be  found  for  the  move- 
ment, so  too  had  a  new  chief.  And,  accordingly, 
before  Luther  had  been  laid  in  his  tomb  at  Wit- 
temberg, Cah-in  was  fairly  in.stalled  at  Geneva. 
He  was  prosecuting  his  work  in  quietness  by  the 
shores  of  the  Leman,  while  the  princes  of  the  Schmal- 
kald League  were  fighting  on  the  plains  of  Germany. 
Under  Calvin  the  Reformation  entered  upon  a  new 
and  more  sjiu-itual  dispensation.  All  the  incidents 
in  Luther's  life  are  sudden,  startling,  and  dramatic : 
this  form  was  given  them  to  draw  attention  and 
fix  the  minds  of  men.  But  the  movement,  once 
launched,  needed  this  array  of  outward  drajiery 
no  longer.  Under  Calvin  it  appeals  less  to  the 
senses  and  more  to  the  intellect :  less  to  the  imagi- 
nation and  more  to  the  soul.  The  evolutions  in 
Calvin's  career  are  quiet,  gradual,  without  the  stage 
efiect,  if  we  may  be  permitted  the  phi-ase,  which 
marked  Liither's  more  notable  appearances,  but  they

Next  in  order  is  the  scheme  of  John  Brentius.

age.  Presbyters â€” that  is.  Councillors.  Tlie  meeting

Synod â€” that  is,  an  assembli/."  Such  was  the  scheme 
of  Brentius ;  it  is  a  well-defined  and  independent 
plan  of  Church  rule,  lodging  the  correction  of 
manners  solelj^  in  the  hands  of  the  Church  herself 
â€” that  is,  of  her  office-bearers. 

Brentius  appeared  on  the  point  of  anticipating

Another  scheme  claims  a  moment's  attention 
from  us.  It  is  that  of  Francis  Lambert,  ex-monk 
â€˘of  Avignon,  and  Philip,  Landgrave  of  Hesse.  It

same  year  (1526)  that  saw  the  scheme  of  John 
Brentius  submitted  to  the  Council  of  Hall.  It  is 
the  most  advanced  of  all.  It  lodged  the  adminis- 
tration of  discipline  immediately  and  du'ectly  in  the 
members  of  tlie  Church.  Firet  of  all,  so  far  as  human 
judgment  could  effect  it,  a  Church  of  saints  only

exclusion  of  scandalous  persons  .  .  .  for  pass- 
ing judgment  on  the  doctrine  of  their  pastor,  for 
electing  and,  in  case  of  need,  dejsosing  bishops  and 
deacons  (i.e.,  ministers  and  helpers)  and  guardians 
of  the  poor,  and  for  whatsoever  other  functions  per- 
tain to  the  congregation ;  for  these  reasons,  we 
ordain  that  in  every  parish,  after  God's  Word  shall 
have  been  preached  for  a  season,  tliere  shall  take 
place  a  convention  of  the  faithful,  wherein  all  males, 
who  favour  the  cause  of  Christ  and  are  reputed 
saints,  shall  come  together  to  decide,  along  with 
the  bishop,  on  all  Chm'ch  afii^ii's,  according  to  the 
Word  of  God.  .  .  .  The  bishop  or  minister 
may  by  no  means  excommunicate  or  absolve  by 
himself,  but  only  in  conjunction  witli  the  congre- 
gation."' 

This  is  not  so  much  the  Presbyterian  as  the 
Congregational  polity.  It  is,  in  fact,  a  scheme 
that  blends  the  two,  for  it  was  made  to  approxi- 
mate the  first,  by  the  institution  of  provincial 
Synods,  consistmg  of  the  pastors  and  a  deputy  from 
every  congi-egation.  It  is  remarkable,  when  its  age 
and  place  are  considered.  A  draft  of  it  was  sent  to 
Luther  for  his  approval.  He  advised  that  for  the 
present  the  project  should  not  be  attempted,  but 
that  every  effort  be  made  to  fill  the  jmlpits  and 
schools  with  efficient  men.  Thereafter  the  plan 
might  be  introduced  piece-meal,  and  if  it  met  with 
general  approval  might  become  law ;  "  for  to  draw 
a  fine  plan  and  to  reduce  it  to  practice  are  two 
â€˘very  different  things.  Men  are  not  consstituted  as 
those  people  imagine  who  sit  at  home  and  sketch 
fine  plans  of  how  things  are  to  go."  This  consti- 
tution was  hardly  set  a-working  when  it  was 
abandoned.     The  Chm-ch  of  Hesse,  surrounded  on

The  plan  of  Zwingle  was  intermediate  between 
that  of  Luther  and  that  of  Calvin.  The  Reformer 
of  Zurich  framed  a  code  of  laws  and  ordinances 
covering  the  enth-e  field  of  social  life,  and  com- 
mitted theii"  administration  to  a  series  of  judges  or 
courts,  supreme  over  which  was  the  State. 

Marriage,  the  Sunday,  and  the  Sacrament  were 
the  three  centres  of  his  moral  scheme,  the  three 
points  on  which  his  ecclesiastical  code  liinged. 
With  Luther,  he  regarded  the  power  of  discipline 
as  vested  in  the  whole  body  of  the  faithful ;  and 
the  provisions  he  made  for  the  exercise  of  that 
power  were,  first,  the  Kirk-session,  or  StUl-stand, 
so  called  for  this  reason,  that  at  the  close  of  public 
worship  the  members  remained  iu  church,  siill- 
staiidini/,  with  the  pastor,  and  in  that  attitude  made 
then-  communications  to  the  minister,  and  to  one 
another,  and  reproved  those  cited  before  them  for 
discipline.-  Secondly,  the  half-yearly  Synod,  which 
chiefly  occupied  itself  with  the  doctrines  and  morals 
of  the  clergy ;  and  thirdly,  the  Board  of  Moral  Con- 
trol, to  which  was  added,  when  the  discipline  of  the 
Church  extended,  the  magistrates  of  the  district. 
Excommunication â€” that  is,  exclusion  from  the  mem- 
bership of  the  Church,  with  all  imjilied  in  tJiat 
sentence  in  Switzerland â€” was  often  pronounced  by 
the  Still-stand  as  a  temporary  measure ;  but  as  a 
final  measure  it  could  be  pronoimced  only  by  the 
Council.  The  supreme  ecclesiastical  authority  was 
thus  in  the  hands  of  the  State,  but  it  was  handed 
over  to  it  by  Zwingle  on  the  express  condition 
that  the  magistrates  were  Christian  men,  and  were 
to  take  the  Word  of  God  as  their  sole  directory 
in  aU  their  proceedings.^  The  zeal  and  prompti- 
tude with  which  the  Council  of  Zurich  aided 
Zwingle  in  his  reformmg  measures,  was  not  with- 
out its  influence  in  moulding  his  scheme  of  polit}^, 
and  indeed  the  Swiss  magistrates  of  those  days 
were  amongst  the  more  enlightened  and  piotis  of 
the  population.  But  seeing  Constitutions  are 
permanent  while  men  change,  in  order  to  be  wisely 
framed  they  ought  to  be  based,  not  on  excejitional 
cases,  but  on  great  and  general  laws. 

Next  to  the  doctrine  of  the  Church,  there  is 
nothing  that  appertams  more  to  her  well-being 
than  her  discipline.  Without  this,  her  life  would 
ebb  away,  and  she  would  fall  back  into  the  world 
from  which  she  had  come  out;  whereas,  with  a 
suitable   organisation,  not  only  would  her  life  be
